network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a highly convergent prebiotic target produced by ten literature-grounded routes centered on five hub molecules: hydrogen cyanide, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks such as formate, formaldehyde, and pyruvate under serpentinizing and iron-sulfur catalytic conditions; surface environments generate and concentrate cyanide chemistry, nitriles, and eutectic HCN polymer reservoirs; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slower hydrolysis, carbamoyl, and assembly steps. The network follows the supplied literature by prioritizing Strecker synthesis, Bucherer-Bergs detours, HCN polymer hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile formation, Fischer-Tropsch-type precursor generation, and ferroan brucite-mediated glyoxylate-to-glycine chemistry, while avoiding unsupported pyruvate-to-glyoxylate shortcuts.

molecules.iupac_name: methanimidic acid nitrile oligomer precursor ensemble

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.pressure: alkaline vent to 25 bar equivalent hydrothermal systems
conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed CO2 hydrogenation on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide surfaces yields formate as a primary reduced C1 product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO2 + H2 reduction to formate on greigite and magnetite under alkaline hydrothermal conditions, with formate as a dominant product; this is a core hydrothermal source of reduced carbon feeding later surface Strecker chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal precursor-pool generation of formaldehyde from reduced C1 carbon

conditions.other: serpentinizing redox gradients; represented as reduced-C1 pool transfer rather than a single selective step

reactions.mechanism: Reduced C1 species generated in hydrothermal systems are transferred into a formaldehyde-accessible precursor pool under mineral redox catalysis.
reactions.reasoning: The literature strongly supports hydrothermal production of reduced C1 compounds including formate and methanol rather than a single clean formate-to-formaldehyde reaction; this node is included as a conservative precursor-pool abstraction connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface Strecker chemistry, consistent with Preiner et al. (2020) and the review framing that alkaline vents optimize CO2 to C1 compounds feeding later chemistry. It is partially speculative as a network-connecting availability step.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2

conditions.other: alkaline serpentinization context; pyruvate reported at 100C and at 25C/25 bar with awaruite-class catalysts

reactions.mechanism: Multielectron mineral-catalyzed CO2 fixation generates C3 keto acids including pyruvate in serpentinizing systems.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 on hydrothermal minerals, and Beyazay et al. (2023) showed room-temperature CO2 fixation to pyruvate on naturally relevant Ni3Fe catalysts. Pyruvate is retained here as a hydrothermal metabolic hub, but not converted to glyoxylate because that link was not supported in the provided literature.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of HCN in CO-rich reduced gas on metallic surfaces

conditions.catalyst: meteoritic/native iron and FeNi-like metallic surfaces represented by Fe0 and magnetite
conditions.other: CO-rich reducing gas; precursor-generation in hot zones with cooler-zone transfer

reactions.mechanism: High-temperature metallic-surface chemistry in CO-rich reduced gases produces HCN as part of a precursor pool later entering aqueous surface chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) and Schlesinger & Miller (1983) support Fischer-Tropsch-type and CO-containing atmospheric chemistries as productive sources of bioorganic precursors including HCN; the literature frames this as precursor-pool generation rather than a clean one-step selective synthesis, so this reaction is expressed accordingly.

reactions.name: Surface spark/photochemical generation of HCN from atmospheric feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral photochemical surfaces
conditions.other: spark discharge or UV-driven atmospheric chemistry with dissolution into ponds

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric processing forms cyanide that is then captured in surface waters and mineral interfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957), Johnson et al. (2008), Parker et al. (2011), and Schlesinger & Miller (1983) establish HCN as a principal product of spark-discharge and CO-containing atmospheric chemistry underlying glycine formation. TiO2 is included as a plausible photochemical mineral surface required by the schema for surface reactions.

reactions.name: Surface Strecker pre-equilibrium: formaldehyde plus ammonia to aminomethanol

conditions.catalyst: borate and montmorillonite as concentrative surfaces
conditions.other: warm pond or aqueous condensate; low to moderate NH3

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia nucleophilically adds to formaldehyde to form the carbinolamine aminomethanol.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped the aqueous Strecker landscape and identified aminomethanol as the first intermediate formed from NH3 attack on formaldehyde; this reaction is the literature-defined entry to the dominant glycine pathway.

reactions.name: Surface dehydration of aminomethanol to methanimine

conditions.other: dehydrating microenvironments during wet-dry fluctuations

reactions.mechanism: Carbinolamine dehydration yields the imine methanimine, the electrophilic Strecker substrate for cyanide addition.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) describe aminomethanol dehydration to methanimine as a core mechanistic step in glycinonitrile formation; surface concentration and partial drying are consistent with prebiotic pond conditions.

reactions.name: Surface cyanide addition to methanimine forming aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: clay concentration and cyanide-compatible mineral surfaces
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; glycinonitrile formation barrier ca. 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds to methanimine to form glycinonitrile/aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) explicitly identified glycinonitrile formation from methanimine and cyanide with a rate-limiting barrier near 15 kcal/mol for this section of the pathway. Miller (1957) also identified Strecker chemistry as underlying spark-synthesis amino acid production.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis; nitrile hydration barrier ca. 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile undergoes nitrile hydration to the amide glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped aminoacetonitrile hydration to glycinamide as the next hydrolytic step in aqueous glycine synthesis, with a substantial free-energy barrier that justifies assigning it to prolonged prebiotic processing.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: slow aqueous amide hydrolysis; highest Strecker barrier ca. 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Glycinamide hydrolyzes to aminoacetic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified the final amide hydrolysis from glycinamide to glycine as the highest-barrier step in the canonical Strecker sequence, making this an important bottleneck in the network.

reactions.name: Surface formation of glycolonitrile from formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: pH around 8; HCN and formaldehyde around 1 g/L in Taillades conditions

reactions.mechanism: Cyanohydrin formation from formaldehyde and cyanide produces glycolonitrile, a major sink competing with aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed that under realistic prebiotic conditions glycolonitrile is formed overwhelmingly relative to aminoacetonitrile, making it an essential branch point in any literature-faithful glycine network.

reactions.name: Surface conversion of aminoacetonitrile and CO2 to hydantoin

conditions.other: room temperature, bicarbonate-rich conditions

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile reacts in the presence of CO2/bicarbonate to form the cyclic ureide hydantoin via Bucherer-Bergs-type carbamoylation and cyclization.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) emphasized the productive role of CO2 in diverting aminoacetonitrile into hydantoin under prebiotic conditions, and Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated high hydantoin yields in bicarbonate-buffered systems, validating the relevance of this branch.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis converts hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) identify hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine as the key intermediate step in the Bucherer-Bergs route to glycine.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis/decarbamoylation

reactions.mechanism: N-carbamoylglycine hydrolyzes and decarbamoylates to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) established N-carbamoylglycine as the immediate Bucherer-Bergs precursor to glycine under prebiotic conditions.

reactions.name: Surface eutectic concentration of HCN into ammonium cyanide reservoir

conditions.other: freezing to around -23.4C eutectic concentrates HCN dramatically

reactions.mechanism: HCN and ammonia are concentrated in freezing brines as ammonium cyanide-rich reservoirs, enabling chemistry impossible in dilute oceans.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967), Levy et al. (2000), and Menor-Salvan & Marin-Yaseli (2012) show eutectic freezing as a key concentration mechanism for HCN-derived chemistry. This reaction is represented as a reservoir-forming concentration step rather than a discrete covalent synthesis.

reactions.name: Surface polymerization of concentrated HCN reservoir to HCN oligomer pool

conditions.other: productive only above approximately 0.01-0.1 M HCN; freezing and concentration required

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated ammonium cyanide/HCN undergoes oligomerization to a heterogeneous polymer/oligomer ensemble.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) quantified HCN polymerization kinetics and concentration thresholds, while Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) and Oró's earlier work established that hydrolysis of these polymers yields glycine prominently. The oligomer node is intentionally treated as an ensemble, not as a falsely specific molecule.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of HCN oligomer pool to glycine-rich amino acid mixture

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis of polymer ensemble; product is represented by the target glycine component

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of heterogeneous HCN oligomers releases amino acids, with glycine consistently among the dominant products.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid under all 23 tested HCN polymer hydrolysis conditions, and Matthews & Moser (1966) plus Oró's work support glycine emergence from HCN polymer reservoirs. This remains an ensemble process and is represented as such.

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry generating aminoacetonitrile precursor pool

conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S reductant, phosphate buffer, Cu photoredox context from literature

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes UV-driven reductive homologation in the presence of sulfide and phosphate to give an aminonitrile-bearing precursor network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) showed that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at about 35C generates amino acid precursors, ribonucleotide precursors, and lipid precursors simultaneously. This node captures the literature's systems-chemistry precursor pool without overcompressing individual undocumented intermediates.

reactions.name: Surface maturation of cyanosulfidic precursor pool to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: continued UV/redox processing in phosphate-buffered water

reactions.mechanism: The cyanosulfidic network resolves into aminonitrile products including aminoacetonitrile under continued photoredox and ammonia-accessible conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) specifically identified aminoacetonitrile as a glycine precursor produced within unified cyanosulfidic chemistry. This step is therefore included as a literature-grounded route into the canonical hydrolysis branch.

reactions.name: Surface photoaqueous conversion of HCN and water to formamide pool

conditions.other: UV irradiation in shallow ponds or films

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical hydration/oxidation states of cyanide generate formamide-accessible pools in irradiated aqueous environments.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature notes UV photochemistry of HCN and water to formamide as a recognized surface process, and Senanayake & Idriss (2006) demonstrated TiO2 photocatalysis of formamide to prebiotic products. This node stops at formamide and avoids unsupported direct formamide-to-glycine claims.

reactions.name: Surface photochemical conversion of formamide pool into glycine-accessible precursor pool

conditions.other: UV irradiation; heterogeneous organic product mixture

reactions.mechanism: UV/TiO2 processing of formamide generates a mixed prebiotic organic precursor ensemble relevant to amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Senanayake & Idriss (2006) showed TiO2-assisted conversion of formamide to prebiotic organics including amino-acid-related products, but the supplied glycine literature does not support a clean direct glycine step here. Therefore this is conservatively represented as a precursor-pool formation node.

reactions.name: Biochemical resolution of photo-organic precursor pool to glycine

conditions.other: mixture-resolving hydrolysis and aging

reactions.mechanism: Complex photo-generated organics are hydrolyzed and reorganized over time, releasing glycine among resolved products.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative but necessary to connect the literature-supported formamide/TiO2 photochemical organic pool to the target. It is justified by the broad product scope reported by Senanayake & Idriss (2006) and by the review's statement that UV/formamide pathways can yield amino acids, but the exact glycine-selective route is unresolved.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal production and sequestration of glyoxylate in serpentinizing mineral systems

conditions.other: serpentinite system; glyoxylate availability represented because it is the documented substrate in the 2024 brucite system

reactions.mechanism: Serpentinization-associated mineral environments provide and retain glyoxylate as a reactive C2 ketoacid substrate on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: The provided literature strongly supports glyoxylate as the substrate for ferroan brucite-mediated glycine synthesis in serpentinite systems (PNAS 2024, doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121), but does not define a single universally accepted synthetic route to glyoxylate in those systems. This node is therefore an availability/sequestration step grounded in the documented mineral context and explicitly conservative.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal ferroan brucite-mediated synthesis of glycine from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.other: serpentinite conditions; up to 97% glycine retained on mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Ferroan brucite mediates reductive amination chemistry between nitrate-derived nitrogen and glyoxylate, simultaneously synthesizing and sequestering glycine on the mineral.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) directly demonstrated glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate on ferroan brucite, with most product bound to the mineral. This is one of the strongest non-Strecker glycine routes in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: Surface formaldehyde-cyanide-ammonia one-pot Strecker convergence to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: one-pot network shorthand for the aminomethanol/methanimine/cyanide sequence

reactions.mechanism: Integrated Strecker chemistry converts formaldehyde, ammonia, and cyanide to aminoacetonitrile through the aminomethanol and methanimine intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: This condensed route is included in addition to the explicit stepwise Strecker sequence because Miller (1957) and later Strecker analyses treat the overall conversion as the dominant glycine-forming process, while Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) provide the underlying mechanistic decomposition.

reactions.name: Biochemical direct overall hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: net hydrolysis summarizing glycinamide-mediated conversion

reactions.mechanism: Overall aqueous hydrolysis converts aminoacetonitrile to glycine over time via amide intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: This net reaction summarizes the two-step hydrolysis sequence of Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) for pathway accounting, while the explicit mechanistic path remains captured by rxn_009 and rxn_010.

pathways.name: Canonical stepwise Strecker from hydrothermal formaldehyde supply
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced C1 chemistry supplies formaldehyde, which undergoes the literature-defined aminomethanol -> methanimine -> aminoacetonitrile -> glycinamide -> glycine sequence.

pathways.name: One-pot Strecker from hydrothermal formaldehyde and atmospheric HCN
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde combines with surface-generated HCN and ammonia in a one-pot Strecker route to aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs branch from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is diverted by CO2 into hydantoin and N-carbamoylglycine before hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-competition-fed Strecker convergence
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN first accumulate as glycolonitrile under Taillades-like conditions while a smaller parallel aminoacetonitrile fraction enters the canonical hydrolysis route to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN eutectic polymer reservoir hydrolysis
pathways.description: HCN is concentrated by freezing with ammonia, polymerized above the concentration threshold, and hydrolyzed to a glycine-rich amino acid mixture.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch-fed HCN polymer route
pathways.description: HCN generated in CO-rich high-temperature metallic systems is transferred to surface eutectic reservoirs and ultimately hydrolyzed from HCN oligomers to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route to aminoacetonitrile then glycine
pathways.description: Patel-Sutherland cyanosulfidic chemistry yields aminoacetonitrile precursors that mature to aminoacetonitrile and enter the canonical hydrolysis branch.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: Metal-catalyzed CO-rich chemistry generates HCN while hydrothermal C1 reduction supplies formaldehyde; both converge with ammonia in Strecker chemistry to glycine.

pathways.name: Ferroan brucite glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: Serpentinite-associated ferroan brucite systems provide glyoxylate and directly mediate glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate with mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: Formamide photochemical organic-pool route
pathways.description: HCN is photochemically converted to formamide on TiO2-rich surfaces, which then yields a prebiotic organic pool that resolves to glycine on further aqueous aging.
